title: Find local minimum in array
description: Find an element that is smaller than its neighbors in an array

examples.explanation: Step 1: n = 5, so n > 2, proceed to loop. Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 2, compare with arr[0]=3 and arr[2]=1: 2 < 3? Yes, 2 < 1? No, so continue. Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 1, compare with arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=4: 1 < 2? Yes, 1 < 4? Yes, so return index 2.

Step 1: n = 5, so n > 2, proceed to loop. Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 4, compare with arr[0]=5 and arr[2]=3: 4 < 5? Yes, 4 < 3? No, continue. Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 3, compare with arr[1]=4 and arr[3]=2: 3 < 4? Yes, 3 < 2? No, continue. Step 4: i = 3, arr[3] = 2, compare with arr[2]=3 and arr[4]=1: 2 < 3? Yes, 2 < 1? No, continue. Step 5: Loop ends, return -1 as no local minimum found.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array from index 1 to n-2, checking if each element is smaller than its immediate neighbors. This is straightforward and works for any array but requires scanning all interior elements.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Local Minimum in Array Using Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find any element smaller than both neighbors by scanning the entire array.
ladders.explanation: The linear scan approach solves the local minimum problem by examining each interior element of the array to check if it's smaller than both its immediate neighbors. Intuitively, this works because a local minimum is defined by this exact property: arr[i] < arr[i-1] and arr[i] < arr[i+1]. By systematically checking every possible candidate position (indices 1 through n-2), we guarantee finding one if it exists. The algorithm starts by handling edge cases: if the array has 0 or 1 elements, there's no interior to check, so we return -1 or the single element respectively. For arrays of size 2, we compare the two elements. For larger arrays, we iterate from index 1 to n-2, comparing each element with its neighbors. If found, we return the index; otherwise, we return -1 after the loop. Time complexity is O(n) since we potentially examine all n-2 interior elements in the worst case (e.g., a strictly increasing array). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the full scan logic that uses the neighbor comparison as a subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle arrays of size less than 3, off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., going to n-1 instead of n-2), and not returning -1 when no local minimum exists. Real code pattern: def find_local_minimum(arr): n = len(arr); if n == 0: return -1; if n == 1: return 0; if n == 2: return 0 if arr[0] < arr[1] else 1; for i in range(1, n-1): if arr[i] < arr[i-1] and arr[i] < arr[i+1]: return i; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 5, so n > 2, proceed to loop. Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 2, compare with arr[0]=3 and arr[2]=1: 2 < 3? Yes, 2 < 1? No, so continue. Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 1, compare with arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=4: 1 < 2? Yes, 1 < 4? Yes, so return index 2.

Step 1: n = 5, so n > 2, proceed to loop. Step 2: i = 1, arr[1] = 4, compare with arr[0]=5 and arr[2]=3: 4 < 5? Yes, 4 < 3? No, continue. Step 3: i = 2, arr[2] = 3, compare with arr[1]=4 and arr[3]=2: 3 < 4? Yes, 3 < 2? No, continue. Step 4: i = 3, arr[3] = 2, compare with arr[2]=3 and arr[4]=1: 2 < 3? Yes, 2 < 1? No, continue. Step 5: Loop ends, return -1 as no local minimum found.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Check If Element is Smaller Than Neighbors
ladders.desc: Helper function to determine if a given array element is a local minimum by comparing with adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core check in the linear scan approach: given an array and an index, it verifies whether the element at that index is smaller than both its immediate neighbors. The intuition is that a local minimum is defined by this relational property, so isolating this check allows modular and reusable code. The algorithm takes the array and index i as inputs. First, it validates that i is within the valid range for having two neighbors (i.e., 1 ≤ i ≤ n-2). If i is at the boundaries (0 or n-1), it cannot have both neighbors, so we return False or handle it as a special case. Then, it compares arr[i] with arr[i-1] and arr[i+1] using simple less-than comparisons. If both conditions hold, return True; otherwise, return False. Time complexity is O(1) as it performs a constant number of comparisons and checks. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly in the loop to test each candidate. Common mistakes include not checking array bounds leading to index errors, misunderstanding the definition (e.g., checking ≤ instead of <), and not handling edge indices properly. Real code pattern: def is_local_minimum(arr, i): n = len(arr); if i <= 0 or i >= n-1: return False; return arr[i] < arr[i-1] and arr[i] < arr[i+1].

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 5, i = 2, check if i <= 0? No, i >= n-1? 2 >= 4? No, so proceed. Step 2: Compare arr[2]=1 with arr[1]=2: 1 < 2? Yes. Step 3: Compare arr[2]=1 with arr[3]=4: 1 < 4? Yes. Step 4: Both true, return True.

Step 1: n = 5, i = 1, check if i <= 0? No, i >= n-1? 1 >= 4? No, so proceed. Step 2: Compare arr[1]=4 with arr[0]=5: 4 < 5? Yes. Step 3: Compare arr[1]=4 with arr[2]=3: 4 < 3? No. Step 4: Second comparison false, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through Array Indices to Find Local Minimum
ladders.desc: The central technique of scanning indices from 1 to n-2 to apply the neighbor check.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the iteration logic that drives the linear scan approach. The intuition is that to find a local minimum, we need to examine each potential candidate position in the array, specifically the interior indices where an element has both left and right neighbors. By looping from i = 1 to i = n-2 (inclusive), we cover all such positions systematically. The algorithm involves initializing a loop with these bounds and, for each index, calling the neighbor check function (from L1) or directly performing the comparisons. If a local minimum is found, we break and return the index; if the loop completes without finding one, we return -1. Time complexity is O(n) in the worst case as we may check all n-2 indices. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the check as the loop body and to L0 as the main scanning mechanism. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., starting at 0 or ending at n-1, which would cause index errors), not handling the return value properly, and inefficiently checking elements multiple times. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, len(arr)-1): if arr[i] < arr[i-1] and arr[i] < arr[i+1]: return i.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 5, loop i from 1 to 3. Step 2: i = 1, arr[1]=2, compare with arr[0]=4 and arr[2]=5: 2 < 4? Yes, 2 < 5? Yes, so return index 1.

Step 1: n = 4, loop i from 1 to 2. Step 2: i = 1, arr[1]=2, compare with arr[0]=1 and arr[2]=3: 2 < 1? No, continue. Step 3: i = 2, arr[2]=3, compare with arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=4: 3 < 2? No, continue. Step 4: Loop ends, return -1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Access Array Elements with Indices and Compare Values
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of using indices to retrieve array elements and perform relational comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two key operations: accessing array elements via indices and comparing numerical values using relational operators. The intuition is that to check if an element is a local minimum, we need to retrieve its value and those of its neighbors, then compare them. Accessing arr[i] gives the element at position i, where i is an integer index starting from 0. Comparing uses operators like < (less than) to determine if one value is smaller than another. The algorithm for a simple check: given array arr and index i, compute left = arr[i-1], current = arr[i], right = arr[i+1], then evaluate current < left and current < right. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L2 by being the fundamental operation within the loop. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., accessing i-1 when i=0), misunderstanding zero-based indexing, and incorrect comparison logic (e.g., using ≤ instead of <). Real code pattern: left = arr[i-1]; current = arr[i]; right = arr[i+1]; if current < left and current < right: ...

examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[0] = 5 as left. Step 2: Access arr[1] = 3 as current. Step 3: Access arr[2] = 8 as right. Step 4: Compare 3 < 5: True. Step 5: Compare 3 < 8: True.
Step 1: Access arr[0] = 2 as left. Step 2: Access arr[1] = 4 as current. Step 3: Access arr[2] = 1 as right. Step 4: Compare 4 < 2: False. Step 5: Compare 4 < 1: False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Two Numbers Using Less-Than Operator
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of determining if one number is smaller than another.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using the less-than operator (<) to compare two numerical values. The intuition is that relational comparisons are essential for checking conditions like arr[i] < arr[i-1]. The algorithm is straightforward: given two numbers a and b, evaluate a < b, which returns True if a is numerically smaller than b, and False otherwise. This is a constant-time operation with O(1) time and O(1) space. It connects to L3 as it's used in the comparisons between array elements. Common mistakes include confusing < with ≤ (less than or equal), comparing non-numeric types without proper handling, and misunderstanding operator precedence in complex expressions. Real code pattern: result = a < b.

examples.trace: Step 1: Take a = 3 and b = 5. Step 2: Apply operator <: 3 < 5. Step 3: Evaluate: 3 is less than 5, so return True.
Step 1: Take a = 7 and b = 2. Step 2: Apply operator <: 7 < 2. Step 3: Evaluate: 7 is not less than 2, so return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Prerequisite concept of accessing elements in an array using integer indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level covers the basics of array indexing, which is crucial for the linear scan approach. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via an index. In most programming languages, indexing starts at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To access an element, you use the syntax arr[i], where i is an integer within the range 0 to n-1 (n being the array length). The intuition is that indices act as pointers to specific positions, allowing retrieval and manipulation of values. The algorithm for access is simple: given array arr and index i, return the value stored at position i. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L4 as it enables getting values to compare. Common mistakes include using indices outside the valid range (causing errors), misunderstanding zero-based indexing (e.g., thinking index 1 is the first element), and confusing arrays with other data structures. Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has elements [10, 20, 30]. Step 2: Index 0 corresponds to 10, index 1 to 20, index 2 to 30. Step 3: Access arr[1]: return 20.
Step 1: Array has elements [5, 8, 2, 9]. Step 2: Index 0:5, 1:8, 2:2, 3:9. Step 3: Access arr[3]: return 9.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the array by comparing the middle element with its neighbors; if not a local minimum, move towards the direction of the smaller neighbor. This exploits the property that a local minimum exists in any array with distinct elements.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Local Minimum Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of binary search approach to find any local minimum in an array.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find a local minimum in an array - an element that's smaller than both its immediate neighbors. For edge elements, we only compare with the one existing neighbor. The binary search approach exploits a key property: in any array with distinct elements, a local minimum must exist. The intuition is that by repeatedly moving toward the smaller neighbor, we're guaranteed to find a valley point. Starting with the entire array, we check the middle element. If it's a local minimum, we return it. If not, we look at its neighbors: if the left neighbor is smaller, the local minimum must be in the left half (since the array values are decreasing toward the left from the middle). Similarly, if the right neighbor is smaller, we search the right half. This creates a binary search pattern. The algorithm maintains left and right pointers, calculates mid, checks if arr[mid] is a local minimum by comparing with neighbors (handling edge cases), and otherwise moves left or right based on which neighbor is smaller. Time complexity is O(log n) since we halve the search space each iteration. Space is O(1) as we only use pointers. This connects to L1 by using the neighbor comparison logic to decide search direction. Common mistakes include not handling array edges properly (first/last elements), infinite loops from incorrect pointer updates, and assuming the array must be sorted (it doesn't need to be). The real pattern involves while loops with careful boundary checks.

examples.trace:
Array: [9, 6, 3, 14, 5, 7, 4]
Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3 → arr[3]=14, neighbors: arr[2]=3, arr[4]=5 → 14 > 3 and 14 > 5? No (14 > 3 but 14 > 5 is true? Actually 14 > 5 is true, but need both neighbors smaller for local min. Check: left neighbor 3 < 14, right neighbor 5 < 14 → 14 is not local min. Compare neighbors: left=3, right=5 → 3 < 5, so move left (since smaller neighbor is on left).
Step 2: left=0, right=2, mid=1 → arr[1]=6, neighbors: arr[0]=9, arr[2]=3 → 6 > 9? No (6 < 9), 6 > 3? Yes → not local min. Compare neighbors: left=9, right=3 → 3 < 9, so move right.
Step 3: left=2, right=2, mid=2 → arr[2]=3, neighbors: arr[1]=6, arr[3]=14 → 3 < 6 and 3 < 14 → local minimum found. Return 3.

Array: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2 → arr[2]=3, neighbors: arr[1]=2, arr[3]=4 → 3 > 2 and 3 > 4? No (3 > 2 is true, 3 > 4 is false). Not local min. Compare neighbors: left=2, right=4 → 2 < 4, so move left.
Step 2: left=0, right=1, mid=0 → arr[0]=1, only right neighbor arr[1]=2 → 1 < 2 → local minimum (edge case). Return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare Middle Element and Decide Search Direction
ladders.desc: Determine if middle element is local minimum or choose which half to search next.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the binary search approach. Given an array and indices left, mid, right, we need to: 1) Check if arr[mid] is a local minimum by comparing with its neighbors (handling array boundaries), and 2) If it's not a local minimum, decide whether to search left or right based on which neighbor is smaller. The intuition is that if arr[mid] is not a valley, then at least one neighbor must be smaller. By moving toward the smaller neighbor, we're guaranteed to eventually find a local minimum because we're following a descending path. The algorithm: First, check if mid is at left boundary (mid == left): if yes, compare only with right neighbor; if arr[mid] < arr[mid+1], it's a local minimum. Similarly for right boundary (mid == right): compare with left neighbor. For interior points, compare with both neighbors. If not a local minimum, compare arr[mid-1] and arr[mid+1]: if arr[mid-1] < arr[mid+1], return 'left' to search left half; otherwise return 'right'. Time complexity is O(1) for the comparison. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the decision logic for each binary search iteration. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in neighbor indices, not handling single-element arrays, and incorrect comparison logic (e.g., comparing wrong neighbors). The pattern uses if-elif-else chains with careful index bounds checking.

examples.trace: arr[2]=5, neighbors: arr[1]=2, arr[3]=1 → Check local min: 5 < 2? No → not local min. Compare neighbors: left neighbor arr[1]=2, right neighbor arr[3]=1 → 1 < 2, so right neighbor is smaller → return 'right' to search right half.
arr[1]=3, neighbors: arr[0]=7, arr[2]=4 → Check local min: 3 < 7? Yes, 3 < 4? Yes → both neighbors larger → local minimum found. Return 'found'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Unordered Array
ladders.desc: Apply binary search technique to find a valley in an unsorted array.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the core insight: binary search can work on unsorted arrays if we have a property that guarantees which half contains the solution. For local minimum, the property is: if the middle element is not a local minimum, then at least one neighbor is smaller. By moving toward the smaller neighbor, we maintain the invariant that a local minimum exists in the new search interval. The intuition is similar to finding a root in a continuous function - we follow the downward slope. The algorithm starts with the entire array as search space [left, right]. While left <= right, compute mid = (left + right) // 2. Use the L1 sub-routine to check if arr[mid] is local minimum. If yes, return mid. If the sub-routine says 'left', update right = mid - 1 to search left half. If 'right', update left = mid + 1 to search right half. Time complexity is O(log n) as we halve search space each step. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using its direction output to update pointers. Common mistakes include using the wrong loop condition (should be left <= right for proper termination), incorrect pointer updates, or not handling the case where mid becomes a boundary. The pattern is a standard binary search loop but with a custom decision function.

examples.trace:
left=0, right=4
Iteration 1: mid=2 → arr[2]=6, neighbors: 4 and 2 → not local min, right neighbor 2 < left neighbor 4 → move right: left=3
Iteration 2: left=3, right=4, mid=3 → arr[3]=2, neighbors: 6 and 5 → 2 < 6 and 2 < 5 → local min found at index 3. Wait, check: Actually in iteration 1, we moved right so left=mid+1=3. Then mid=3, arr[3]=2 compares with arr[2]=6 and arr[4]=5 → both larger → local min. Return index 3.

left=0, right=2
Iteration 1: mid=1 → arr[1]=1, neighbors: 3 and 2 → 1 < 3 and 1 < 2 → local min found. Return index 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check Local Minimum Condition
ladders.desc: Determine if a given array element is smaller than its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept checks whether an element at a given index is a local minimum. A local minimum is defined as an element that is smaller than both its immediate neighbors. For edge elements (first or last index), we only compare with the one existing neighbor. The intuition is straightforward: we're looking for valleys in the array landscape. The algorithm takes an array and an index i. First, handle edge cases: if i is 0 (first element), check if arr[0] < arr[1] (if array has at least 2 elements). If i is n-1 (last element), check if arr[n-1] < arr[n-2]. For interior indices (0 < i < n-1), check if arr[i] < arr[i-1] and arr[i] < arr[i+1]. Return True if condition holds, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the local minimum check within binary search. Common mistakes include not handling single-element arrays (which trivially have a local minimum), off-by-one errors in neighbor indices, and incorrect comparison operators (should be < not <= for strict local minimum). The pattern uses conditional checks with careful array bounds verification.

examples.trace: Index 1 is interior (0 < 1 < 4). Compare arr[1]=2 with left neighbor arr[0]=4 → 2 < 4 is True. Compare with right neighbor arr[2]=5 → 2 < 5 is True. Both conditions True → local minimum.
Index 0 is first element. Compare with only right neighbor arr[1]=4 → arr[0]=3 < 4 is True → local minimum.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Array Elements with Neighbors
ladders.desc: Compare an element's value with its adjacent elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing an element's neighbors in an array and performing comparisons. Given an array and an index, we need to safely access arr[i-1] and arr[i+1] when they exist, then compare arr[i] with these values. The intuition is simple array indexing and comparison. The algorithm: First, check if index i is valid (0 <= i < n). Then, initialize comparisons: for left neighbor, if i > 0, access arr[i-1] and compare with arr[i]; otherwise, note that left neighbor doesn't exist. For right neighbor, if i < n-1, access arr[i+1] and compare; otherwise, note it doesn't exist. Perform comparisons using < operator. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the comparison results needed for local minimum check. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors when accessing i-1 for i=0 or i+1 for i=n-1, and not handling empty arrays. The pattern uses if statements to check bounds before accessing.

examples.output: left comparison: 3 < 5 is True, right comparison: 3 < 7 is True
examples.trace: Index 1 is valid. Left neighbor: i=1 > 0, so access arr[0]=5, compare arr[1]=3 < 5 → True. Right neighbor: i=1 < 2, so access arr[2]=7, compare 3 < 7 → True.

examples.output: left comparison: no neighbor, right comparison: 4 < 2 is False
examples.trace: Index 0 is valid. Left neighbor: i=0 not > 0 → no left neighbor. Right neighbor: i=0 < 1, access arr[1]=2, compare arr[0]=4 < 2 → False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and understand neighbor relationships.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to access array elements by their index. An array is a contiguous block of elements where each element has an index starting from 0. To access an element, we use the syntax arr[i] where i is the index. The intuition is like house numbers on a street. Key concepts: the first element is arr[0], the last is arr[n-1] where n is array length. Neighbors of an element at index i are arr[i-1] (left neighbor, if i > 0) and arr[i+1] (right neighbor, if i < n-1). Edge elements have only one neighbor. The algorithm for this level is simply practicing array access: given an array and an index, return the element value. Also, identify valid indices (0 <= i < n). Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing the basic skill needed to compare neighbors. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking first element is arr[1]), accessing out-of-bounds indices, and not checking array length. The pattern is direct array indexing with bounds awareness.

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements: indices 0,1,2. Index 1 is valid. arr[1] = 20. Left neighbor: index 1-1=0 → arr[0]=10. Right neighbor: index 1+1=2 → arr[2]=30.

examples.output: Element: 5, Left neighbor: none, Right neighbor: none
examples.trace: Array has 1 element. Index 0 is valid. arr[0]=5. Left neighbor: index 0-1=-1 is invalid → no left neighbor. Right neighbor: index 0+1=1 is invalid (since length=1) → no right neighbor.
